guide.title: Lesson 3.2 — Protecting Networks: Managerial Controls — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Unit 3 · Topic 3.2 · 3 class periods · Scenario 3B
overview: Lesson 3.2 opens the protective half of Unit 3: after Topic 3.1 catalogued network vulnerabilities and attacks, this topic asks how an organization sets policy to harden its network devices and then configures a wireless network to resist those attacks. Across three days students learn to identify the managerial controls related to network security — router, switch, VPN, and wireless security policies and what each policy specifies (EK 3.2.A.1–A.4) — and to configure the specific wireless security features the CED names: disabling beacon-frame broadcasting, controlling broadcast direction and signal strength, enabling strong encryption (WPA3), and enabling MAC filtering with required authentication (EK 3.2.B.1–B.4). The work is anchored on Scenario 3B, Configuring a Secure Wireless Network, where students describe configurations to secure a wireless network.

The Day 1 organizing idea is the distinction between a managerial control and a technical configuration: a security policy (3.2.A) is a written minimum-standard document that says what must be true, while configuring wireless features (3.2.B) is the hands-on technical act that makes it true. The same item — say, MAC filtering or AES/WPA encryption — appears once as a policy requirement and again as a configuration step, and students should be able to name which layer they are in. Scenario 3B is written in the second person and names no individuals; none is added here.
los: LO 3.2.A — Identify managerial controls related to network security.
LO 3.2.B — Configure wireless network security features.
eks: EK 3.2.A.1 — A router security policy will set forth a minimum configuration standard for routers on an organization's network and may include: banning local user accounts (all router logins must use an approved authentication server); disabling unnecessary services (e.g., Telnet); and requiring a firewall (an organization may opt for a firewall device separate from the router).

EK 3.2.A.2 — A switch security policy will set forth a minimum configuration standard for switches on an organization's network and may include: banning local user accounts (all switch logins must use an approved authentication server); requiring port security to be enabled; and using MAC filtering.

EK 3.2.A.3 — A virtual private network (VPN) policy will detail the minimum security requirements for employees using a VPN to access an organization's internal network, and it may include: a list of roles within the organization that are allowed to use a VPN to access the organization's internal network; authentication requirements for employees using a VPN (e.g., public/private key system or MFA); and a prohibition against split tunneling (also called dual tunneling).

EK 3.2.A.4 — A wireless security policy will establish the minimum security requirements for wireless networks within an organization and may include: requiring users to authenticate to the wireless network through an extensible authentication protocol (EAP) connected to an approved authentication server; requiring all wireless traffic to be encrypted using AES encryption with a minimum key length; and disabling beacon frames on wireless access points.
EK 3.2.B.1 — Organizations can disable beacon frame broadcasting on wireless access points (WAPs) to make it harder for adversaries to find their wireless network and learn its basic properties.
EK 3.2.B.2 — Organizations can control the broadcast direction and signal strength of a WAP so the signal does not extend beyond the physical space the access point is meant to cover.

EK 3.2.B.3 — Organizations should enable strong wireless encryption protocols to ensure wireless frames are not readable by adversaries who might intercept them. WEP, WPS, and the original WPA wireless encryption protocols have known vulnerabilities and are insecure. WPA3 is currently the strongest wireless encryption algorithm.
EK 3.2.B.4 — Organizations can enable MAC filtering to prevent unauthorized devices from accessing the network, and they can require users to authenticate when joining a network.
materials: Projector / slide deck (teacher · deep-dive version) for each day.

Printed/Digital Day 1–3 Guided Notes (student copies). Workflow: students fill the notes in live during the slide presentation; anything missed can be completed — or checked — afterward against the lesson page (apcsexamprep.com/pages/ap-cybersecurity-unit-3-managerial-controls), which carries all the content. The formal answer KEYs stay in this folder and are never posted to the site.
Printed Lesson 3.2 quiz (student copies) for Day 3.
Scenario 3B narrative (Configuring a Secure Wireless Network) displayed on the decks (no handout needed).
prereqs: Topic 3.1 (Network Vulnerabilities and Attacks). Lesson 3.2 is the protective counterpart: 3.1 identified how adversaries exploit a network, and 3.2 sets policy and configures wireless features to defend against them. Students should also recall MAC addresses and authentication-server concepts, which reappear here in switch and wireless policy.

pacing.focus: Identify the four managerial network-security policies (LO 3.2.A)

segments.segment: QOTD (U3/L3.2/Q2) + bell ringer (hiding and shrinking the signal)
Disabling beacon frames + controlling broadcast direction/signal strength (EK 3.2.B.1–B.2)
Strong encryption: WEP/WPS/original WPA vs. WPA3 (EK 3.2.B.3)
MAC filtering + required authentication (EK 3.2.B.4) + Stop and Think

pacing.focus: Apply to Scenario 3B and review the whole topic + quiz (LO 3.2.A, LO 3.2.B)

segments.segment: QOTD (U3/L3.2/Q3) + bell ringer (the Scenario 3B wireless walkthrough)
Worked review: each policy and each wireless configuration, side by side
Configure a secure wireless network for Scenario 3B + Stop and Think

misconceptions.heading: A policy is the same as a configuration
misconceptions.myth: Writing a wireless security policy is enough to protect the network.
misconceptions.reality: LO 3.2.A managerial controls are written minimum-standard documents (router, switch, VPN, wireless policies); LO 3.2.B is the separate, hands-on act of actually configuring the wireless features. A policy that says 'use WPA3' protects nothing until someone configures the WAP. Both layers are required.

misconceptions.heading: All WPA is safe
misconceptions.myth: As long as the wireless network uses WPA, the traffic is secure.
misconceptions.reality: EK 3.2.B.3 says the ORIGINAL WPA, like WEP and WPS, has known vulnerabilities and is insecure. WPA3 is currently the strongest wireless encryption algorithm — the version number matters, not just the WPA name.

misconceptions.heading: Local accounts on devices are fine
misconceptions.myth: Each router or switch should keep its own local login accounts for convenience.
misconceptions.reality: EK 3.2.A.1 and A.2 say router and switch security policies may BAN local user accounts — all device logins must instead use an approved authentication server, so access is centrally controlled and revoked.

misconceptions.heading: Disabling beacon frames is encryption
misconceptions.myth: Turning off beacon-frame broadcasting encrypts the wireless traffic.
misconceptions.reality: EK 3.2.B.1 says disabling beacon frames only makes the network harder to FIND and learn basic properties of — it hides the network, it does not encrypt frames. Encryption is a separate step (EK 3.2.B.3, WPA3).

support: Post a two-column anchor — 'POLICY (3.2.A): router / switch / VPN / wireless — a written rule' vs. 'CONFIGURE (3.2.B): beacon frames / signal direction / WPA3 / MAC filtering — a hands-on action' — so students sort each item into the right layer before the Stop and Think.
Provide a sentence frame: 'This is EK 3.2.____ because it is a ____ (policy / configuration) that ____.'
extension: Have students take a single requirement (e.g., MAC filtering or AES encryption) and trace it from the policy that requires it (3.2.A) to the configuration that implements it (3.2.B), explaining the role of each layer.
Ask students to justify, using EK 3.2.B.3, why an organization still running original WPA is at risk even though it technically has 'wireless encryption,' and what single change most improves it.

discussion: Why does the CED separate managerial controls (3.2.A) from configuring wireless features (3.2.B) — what does each layer add that the other cannot?
Router and switch policies both 'ban local user accounts.' What does requiring an approved authentication server buy an organization that local accounts do not?
EK 3.2.B.2 controls signal direction and strength. How is shrinking a wireless signal to the building footprint a security control, not just a performance tweak?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is pulled straight from the quiz on this page, so it always matches the questions students see.

quiz.title: Lesson 3.2 — Protecting Networks: Managerial Controls — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 8 questions, 15 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: An organization is writing a document that sets a minimum configuration standard for the routers on its network, including a requirement that all router logins use an approved authentication server and that unnecessary services such as Telnet be disabled. Which managerial control is the organization creating?
options: A router security policy, which sets the minimum configuration standard for the organization's routers
A VPN policy, which lists the roles allowed to remotely access the internal network
A wireless security policy, which sets the minimum standard for the organization's wireless networks
A switch security policy, which requires port security and MAC filtering on switches
questions.rationale: EK 3.2.A.1 says a router security policy sets a minimum configuration standard for routers and may include banning local user accounts (all logins must use an approved authentication server), disabling unnecessary services such as Telnet, and requiring a firewall — exactly the document described. Option B is the VPN policy (EK 3.2.A.3), which governs remote VPN access, not router standards. Option C is the wireless policy (EK 3.2.A.4). Option D is the switch policy (EK 3.2.A.2), which covers switches, not routers.

questions.stem: A switch security policy is being drafted from the CED standard. Per EK 3.2.A.2, which set of requirements belongs in a switch security policy?
options: A prohibition against split tunneling and a list of roles allowed to use a VPN
Banning local user accounts, requiring port security to be enabled, and using MAC filtering
Disabling beacon frames and requiring all wireless traffic to use AES encryption
Requiring a firewall device and disabling unnecessary services such as Telnet on the switch
questions.rationale: EK 3.2.A.2 says a switch security policy may include banning local user accounts (all switch logins must use an approved authentication server), requiring port security to be enabled, and using MAC filtering — option B. Option A lists VPN-policy items (EK 3.2.A.3). Option C lists wireless-policy items (EK 3.2.A.4). Option D lists router-policy items (EK 3.2.A.1); the CED assigns the firewall and Telnet requirements to the router policy, not the switch policy.

questions.stem: A company lets some employees connect to its internal network from home. It wants a policy that names which roles may connect remotely, requires MFA to authenticate, and forbids split tunneling. Per EK 3.2.A.3, which managerial control fits this need?
options: A switch security policy, since switches forward all of the remote traffic on the internal network
A router security policy, since the router is what forwards remote traffic into the network
A VPN policy, which details the minimum security requirements for employees using a VPN to reach the internal network
A wireless security policy, since employees connecting from home are using a wireless connection
questions.rationale: EK 3.2.A.3 says a VPN policy details the minimum security requirements for employees using a VPN to access the internal network and may include a list of allowed roles, authentication requirements such as a public/private key system or MFA, and a prohibition against split tunneling (dual tunneling) — every element described. Options A and B name device policies that govern switch and router configuration standards, not remote VPN access. Option D misreads the scenario — the control governs VPN use, not the wireless medium the employee happens to use at home.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to make the organization's wireless network harder for an adversary to find and learn the basic properties of, without yet changing how the traffic is encrypted. Per EK 3.2.B.1, which configuration achieves this?
options: Upgrading the wireless encryption protocol from original WPA to WPA3 on the access point
Enabling MAC filtering so that only known device hardware addresses can join
Reducing the broadcast signal strength so the signal stays inside the building
Disabling beacon frame broadcasting on the wireless access points
questions.rationale: EK 3.2.B.1 says organizations can disable beacon frame broadcasting on WAPs to make it harder for adversaries to find the wireless network and learn its basic properties — which matches the goal exactly. Option A is an encryption change (EK 3.2.B.3), which the question explicitly set aside. Option B controls which devices may join (EK 3.2.B.4), not discoverability. Option C limits where the signal reaches (EK 3.2.B.2) but does not hide the network's beacon advertisement.

questions.stem: A wireless network's signal currently reaches the parking lot and the sidewalk outside the building, where anyone can attempt to connect. The security team wants to limit how far the signal travels. Per EK 3.2.B.2, what should they configure?
options: Control the WAP's broadcast direction and signal strength so the signal does not extend beyond the space the access point is meant to cover
Disable beacon frames so the network no longer advertises its name to the parking lot
Switch the encryption protocol to WPA3 so outsiders in the parking lot cannot read the frames
Enable MAC filtering so devices in the parking lot are not allowed to join the network
questions.rationale: EK 3.2.B.2 says organizations can control the broadcast direction and signal strength of a WAP so the signal does not extend beyond the physical space the access point is meant to cover — directly addressing a signal that spills into the parking lot. Option B hides the network's name (EK 3.2.B.1) but does not shrink the signal's reach. Option C encrypts frames (EK 3.2.B.3) but still lets the signal reach outsiders. Option D blocks unknown devices (EK 3.2.B.4) but does not reduce signal coverage.

questions.stem: A school audits its wireless network and finds the access points are still using the original WPA encryption protocol. Per EK 3.2.B.3, what is the best assessment and fix?
options: Original WPA is the strongest available protocol, so no change is needed at this time
Original WPA has known vulnerabilities and is insecure, so the network should move to WPA3, currently the strongest wireless encryption algorithm
Original WPA only needs MAC filtering added to it to become a secure encryption protocol
Beacon frames should be disabled to encrypt the original WPA traffic going forward
questions.rationale: EK 3.2.B.3 says WEP, WPS, and the original WPA have known vulnerabilities and are insecure, and that WPA3 is currently the strongest wireless encryption algorithm — so the fix is to move to WPA3. Option A is false; original WPA is insecure. Option B confuses MAC filtering (device control, EK 3.2.B.4) with encryption strength. Option C confuses disabling beacon frames (hiding the network, EK 3.2.B.1) with encryption — neither encrypts traffic.

questions.stem: An administrator wants to stop unauthorized devices from joining the wireless network even if someone learns the password, and also require users to authenticate when joining. Per EK 3.2.B.4, which configuration meets both goals?
options: Reduce the access point's signal strength so unauthorized devices are physically out of range
Disable beacon frame broadcasting so unauthorized devices cannot discover the network
Enable MAC filtering to block unauthorized devices and require users to authenticate when joining
Upgrade the encryption protocol to WPA3 so unauthorized devices cannot read the traffic
questions.rationale: EK 3.2.B.4 says organizations can enable MAC filtering to prevent unauthorized devices from accessing the network and can require users to authenticate when joining — covering both stated goals. Option A limits range (EK 3.2.B.2) but does not block a device that is in range. Option B hides the network (EK 3.2.B.1) but does not stop a device that already knows it. Option D protects confidentiality of traffic (EK 3.2.B.3) but does not by itself control which devices may join.

questions.stem: A wireless security policy is being written from the CED standard. I. Require users to authenticate to the wireless network through an extensible authentication protocol (EAP) connected to an approved authentication server. II. Require all wireless traffic to be encrypted using AES encryption with a minimum key length. III. Require a firewall device that is separate from the router. Which of these belong in a wireless security policy per EK 3.2.A.4?
options: III only (the firewall requirement)
I and III only (EAP authentication and the firewall)
II and III only (AES encryption and the firewall)
I and II only (EAP authentication and AES encryption)
questions.rationale: EK 3.2.A.4 says a wireless security policy may include requiring users to authenticate through an EAP connected to an approved authentication server, requiring all wireless traffic to be encrypted using AES encryption with a minimum key length, and disabling beacon frames on access points — so statements I and II belong. Statement III, requiring a separate firewall device, is part of the ROUTER security policy (EK 3.2.A.1), not the wireless policy. The answer is I and II only.

slides.title: The Rule and the Switch
slides.subtitle: Protecting Networks: the managerial controls that set the minimum security standard for routers, switches, VPNs, and wireless — and the wireless features you actually configure to enforce them
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Unit 3, Securing Networks, and Lesson 3.2, Protecting Networks: Managerial Controls. Topic 3.1 catalogued how adversaries attack a network; this topic flips to defense. There are two halves. First, learning objective 3.2.A: the managerial controls — the router, switch, VPN, and wireless security policies that write down the minimum standard a network must meet. Second, learning objective 3.2.B: actually configuring the wireless features that make a network resist attack. Keep the title in mind all class — there is the rule, the written policy, and the switch you flip to enforce it, and you should always know which one you are looking at.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Identify the four managerial network-security policies — router, switch, VPN, and wireless — and name what each one specifies (LO 3.2.A)
Configure the wireless security features the CED names — disabling beacon frames, controlling signal direction and strength, enabling WPA3, and MAC filtering with required authentication (LO 3.2.B)
Tell a written policy (3.2.A) apart from the hands-on configuration that enforces it (3.2.B), and cite the EK for each

slides.script: Three things to walk out with. First, learning objective 3.2.A: identify the four managerial controls — the router, switch, VPN, and wireless security policies — and what each one specifies. Second, learning objective 3.2.B: configure the wireless features the College Board names, from disabling beacon frames to enabling WPA3 to MAC filtering. Third, and this is the skill the whole topic tests: tell a policy apart from a configuration, and cite the right EK for each. A policy is a written rule; a configuration is the action that makes it real.

slides.prompt: Your school writes a one-page document that says: every router must log in through the central authentication server, Telnet must be turned off, and a firewall is required. Nobody has touched a single router yet — the document just exists in a binder.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: Is the network actually any safer the moment that document is signed? What still has to happen, and who has to do it, before the routers really meet the standard the document describes?
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own, no notes. A school writes a document: every router logs in through the central authentication server, Telnet off, firewall required. But nobody has touched a router — it is just words in a binder. In three to five sentences: is the network safer the instant that document is signed? What still has to happen, and who has to do it? Hold your answers. That gap between the written rule and the configured device is the entire point of today's lesson — policy is 3.2.A, configuration is 3.2.B, and you need both.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes have four sections that match today’s lesson. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: The Four Managerial Controls
sections.description: Router, switch, VPN, and wireless security policies — each a written document setting the minimum security standard, and what each one specifies (EK 3.2.A.1–A.4).

sections.title: Configuring the Wireless Network
sections.description: The four wireless features you actually turn on: disabling beacon frames, controlling signal direction and strength, enabling WPA3, and MAC filtering with required authentication (EK 3.2.B.1–B.4).

sections.title: Policy vs. Configuration
sections.description: Key vocabulary and the one skill the exam keeps testing — sorting each item into the written rule (3.2.A) or the hands-on action (3.2.B) that enforces it.

sections.title: Enrichment: One Requirement, Two Layers
sections.description: Trace a single requirement — like AES/WPA encryption or MAC filtering — from the policy that demands it to the configuration that delivers it (EK 3.2.A.2, A.4, B.3, B.4).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 3.2 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Four sections today. Section one, the four managerial controls: router, switch, VPN, and wireless policies, and what each writes down. Section two, configuring the wireless network: the four features the College Board names, from hiding the network to encrypting it. Section three, the policy-versus-configuration skill, plus your key vocabulary. And section four is a blue enrichment, where we trace one requirement across both layers. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end of class.

slides.label: The Four Managerial Controls
slides.script: Section one. Learning objective 3.2.A: identify the managerial controls related to network security. A managerial control here means a written security policy — a document that sets a minimum standard. The College Board names four of them, one per device or service: router, switch, VPN, and wireless. We will take them in two pairs.

slides.heading: Device Policies: Router and Switch
slides.subheading: EK 3.2.A.1 and A.2 — each sets a minimum configuration standard for that device, and both can ban local accounts in favor of a central authentication server.

left.title: Router Security Policy (EK 3.2.A.1)
examples: Sets a minimum configuration standard for the routers on the network
May ban local user accounts — all router logins must use an approved authentication server
May require disabling unnecessary services, e.g., Telnet
May require a firewall (possibly a firewall device separate from the router)

right.title: Switch Security Policy (EK 3.2.A.2)
examples: Sets a minimum configuration standard for the switches on the network
May ban local user accounts — all switch logins must use an approved authentication server
May require port security to be enabled
May require using MAC filtering

slides.footer: AP TIP: Both device policies can BAN local accounts and route logins through an approved authentication server. The split to remember: the firewall and Telnet requirements live in the ROUTER policy (EK 3.2.A.1); port security and MAC filtering live in the SWITCH policy (EK 3.2.A.2).
slides.script: Two device policies, side by side. On the left, EK 3.2.A.1, the router security policy: it sets a minimum configuration standard for routers, and it may ban local user accounts so every login goes through an approved authentication server, disable unnecessary services like Telnet, and require a firewall — possibly a separate firewall device. On the right, EK 3.2.A.2, the switch security policy: same idea for switches, may also ban local accounts, and adds requiring port security and using MAC filtering. The exam loves to swap these — remember the firewall and Telnet belong to the router policy, port security and MAC filtering to the switch policy. Write both EKs.

slides.heading: Access Policies: VPN and Wireless
slides.subheading: EK 3.2.A.3 and A.4 — these govern how people reach the network remotely and over the air.

left.title: VPN Policy (EK 3.2.A.3)
left.definition: Minimum security requirements for VPN access
examples: Details the minimum security requirements for employees using a VPN to reach the internal network
May list which roles are allowed to use a VPN to access the internal network
May set authentication requirements, e.g., a public/private key system or MFA
May prohibit split tunneling (also called dual tunneling)

right.title: Wireless Security Policy (EK 3.2.A.4)
right.definition: Minimum security requirements for wireless networks
examples: Establishes the minimum security requirements for the organization's wireless networks
May require authentication through an EAP connected to an approved authentication server
May require all wireless traffic encrypted with AES encryption at a minimum key length
May require disabling beacon frames on wireless access points

slides.footer: AP TIP: The VPN policy is about WHO may connect remotely and HOW (roles, MFA, no split tunneling). The wireless policy is about the requirements for the WIRELESS medium (EAP authentication, AES encryption, disabled beacon frames). Note the wireless policy points straight at the 3.2.B features.
slides.script: Two access policies now. On the left, EK 3.2.A.3, the VPN policy: it details the minimum security requirements for employees using a VPN to reach the internal network — it may list which roles are allowed to connect, set authentication requirements like a public/private key system or MFA, and prohibit split tunneling, also called dual tunneling. On the right, EK 3.2.A.4, the wireless security policy: it establishes the minimum requirements for wireless networks — authenticate through an EAP tied to an approved authentication server, encrypt all traffic with AES at a minimum key length, and disable beacon frames. Notice the wireless policy is the bridge to section two: every requirement it names is a feature you will configure next.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 3.2.A)

terms.definition: A written security policy that sets a minimum standard the network must meet, such as the router, switch, VPN, or wireless security policies (EK 3.2.A.1).
A document setting the minimum configuration standard for routers, which may ban local accounts, disable services like Telnet, and require a firewall (EK 3.2.A.1).
A document setting the minimum configuration standard for switches, which may ban local accounts, require port security, and use MAC filtering (EK 3.2.A.2).
A document detailing the minimum security requirements for employees using a VPN to reach the internal network — allowed roles, authentication, and no split tunneling (EK 3.2.A.3).
Routing some traffic through the VPN and some directly to the internet at once; a VPN policy may prohibit it (also called dual tunneling) (EK 3.2.A.3).
A document establishing the minimum security requirements for wireless networks — EAP authentication, AES encryption, and disabled beacon frames (EK 3.2.A.4).

slides.script: Six vocabulary terms for the managerial half. A managerial control is a written security policy setting a minimum standard — the umbrella term. Then the four policies: router, switch, VPN, and wireless, each with the items it may specify. And one term inside the VPN policy worth its own line: split tunneling, also called dual tunneling, routing some traffic through the VPN and some straight to the internet — a VPN policy may prohibit it. Get all six into your notes with their EK numbers; the exam asks you to match a described document to its policy name.

slides.label: Configuring the Wireless Network
slides.script: Section two. Learning objective 3.2.B: configure wireless network security features. This is the hands-on half — the actual settings on a wireless access point that the wireless policy from section one demanded. Four features, in two groups: first hide and shrink the signal, then encrypt it and control which devices may join.

slides.heading: Hide It and Shrink It: Beacon Frames and Signal Reach
slides.subheading: EK 3.2.B.1 and B.2 — two configurations that make the network harder to find and keep its signal inside the building.

items.title: Disable Beacon Frame Broadcasting
items.summary: Disabling beacon-frame broadcasting on wireless access points makes it harder for adversaries to find the wireless network and learn its basic properties. It hides the network — it does not encrypt the traffic.

items.title: Control Direction and Signal Strength
items.summary: Controlling a WAP's broadcast direction and signal strength keeps the signal from extending beyond the physical space the access point is meant to cover, so it does not spill into a parking lot or sidewalk where outsiders could reach it.

slides.script: First two wireless features, both about reducing exposure. EK 3.2.B.1, disabling beacon-frame broadcasting on the access points: a beacon frame is how a network advertises itself, so turning it off makes the network harder for adversaries to find and learn the basic properties of. Important — this hides the network, it does not encrypt anything. EK 3.2.B.2, controlling broadcast direction and signal strength: shrink and aim the signal so it does not extend beyond the space the access point is meant to cover. If your wireless reaches the parking lot, an attacker can sit out there; pulling the signal inside the building is a genuine security control, not just a performance tweak. Write both EKs with a one-line tell.

slides.heading: Encrypt It and Gate It: WPA3 and MAC Filtering
slides.subheading: EK 3.2.B.3 and B.4 — make intercepted frames unreadable, and control which devices may join.

items.title: Enable Strong Encryption (WPA3)
items.summary: Enable strong wireless encryption so frames are not readable by adversaries who intercept them. WEP, WPS, and the original WPA have known vulnerabilities and are insecure; WPA3 is currently the strongest wireless encryption algorithm.

items.title: MAC Filtering + Required Authentication
items.summary: Enable MAC filtering to prevent unauthorized devices from accessing the network, and require users to authenticate when joining. The first controls which device hardware may connect; the second controls which users may.

slides.script: The second two wireless features, both about control of access. EK 3.2.B.3, strong encryption: enable it so frames an adversary intercepts are unreadable. Memorize the version history — WEP, WPS, and the original WPA all have known vulnerabilities and are insecure, and WPA3 is currently the strongest wireless encryption algorithm, so 'we use WPA' is not enough; the number matters. EK 3.2.B.4, MAC filtering plus required authentication: MAC filtering prevents unauthorized devices from joining by hardware address, and requiring authentication controls which users may join. One gates the device, the other gates the person. Write both EKs.

slides.heading: Four Wireless Features, Four Different Jobs
slides.context: Each wireless configuration in EK 3.2.B addresses a different threat. Matching the feature to the job it does is exactly how the exam asks it.
rows: Hides the network so it is harder to find
Keeps the signal inside the building
Does not hide the name or encrypt
Does not control which devices join
Does not shrink or hide the signal
slides.script: Here is the whole second half on one table — the trap the exam sets is treating these four as interchangeable, when each does a different job. Disabling beacon frames, 3.2.B.1, hides the network but encrypts nothing. Controlling direction and signal strength, 3.2.B.2, keeps the signal in the building but does not hide the name. WPA3, 3.2.B.3, makes intercepted frames unreadable but does not decide which devices may join. MAC filtering and required authentication, 3.2.B.4, blocks unknown devices and checks users but does nothing to the signal's reach. Read across each row, then cover the right two columns and quiz yourself.

slides.heading: A Policy Is Not a Configuration
slides.misconception: Writing a wireless security policy that says “use WPA3” protects the network.
slides.correction: A managerial control (3.2.A) is a written rule; it protects nothing until someone performs the configuration (3.2.B) that enforces it. A policy that says “use WPA3” does nothing until an administrator actually enables WPA3 on the access point. Both layers are required.
slides.whyItHappens: Policies and configurations name the same items — WPA/AES encryption, MAC filtering — so students assume writing the rule is the same as doing the work.
slides.example: EK 3.2.A.4 (wireless policy) may REQUIRE AES encryption; EK 3.2.B.3 is the act of actually enabling WPA3 on the WAP. Same goal, two different layers.
slides.script: The single most important misconception in this topic. Students read a wireless policy that says 'use WPA3' and think the network is protected. It is not. A managerial control is a written rule — it protects nothing until someone does the configuration that enforces it. The policy that says 'use WPA3' does absolutely nothing until an administrator enables WPA3 on the access point. The reason this trips people up is that both layers name the same items — WPA, AES, MAC filtering — so the policy and the configuration look identical on paper. They are not. 3.2.A is the rule; 3.2.B is the action; you need both.

slides.heading: Enrichment: One Requirement, Two Layers
slides.subheading: Not a new EK — it traces a single requirement from the policy that demands it to the configuration that delivers it.

left.title: The Policy Layer (3.2.A)
left.definition: The written rule that requires it
examples: AES encryption is REQUIRED by the wireless security policy (EK 3.2.A.4)
MAC filtering is REQUIRED by the switch security policy (EK 3.2.A.2)
The policy states the minimum standard but performs no action
It says what must be true, not how to make it true

right.title: The Configuration Layer (3.2.B)
right.definition: The hands-on act that delivers it
examples: Enabling WPA3 on the WAP is the encryption configuration (EK 3.2.B.3)
Enabling MAC filtering on the wireless network is the device-control configuration (EK 3.2.B.4)
The configuration is the action that makes the standard real
Same requirement, now actually enforced on the device

slides.footer: TAKEAWAY: Pick any requirement and you can trace it twice — once as a policy line (3.2.A) and once as a configuration step (3.2.B). Naming which layer you are in is the skill 3.2 is built to test.
slides.script: A quick enrichment that ties the whole lesson together. Take one requirement — AES, or WPA, encryption — and trace it across both layers. On the left, the policy layer: the wireless security policy, EK 3.2.A.4, requires AES encryption; the switch policy, EK 3.2.A.2, requires MAC filtering. The policy states the standard but performs no action. On the right, the configuration layer: enabling WPA3 on the access point, EK 3.2.B.3, is the encryption in action; enabling MAC filtering, EK 3.2.B.4, is the device control in action. Same requirement, named twice, once as a written rule and once as a configured setting. Being able to say which layer you are in is exactly what this topic tests.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 3.2.A and LO 3.2.B. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: An administrator wants to make the wireless network harder to find, keep its signal inside the building, make intercepted traffic unreadable, and block unknown devices. Name the FOUR wireless configurations that achieve these goals.

For each of these items, state whether it is a managerial control or a configuration: (a) a document requiring all switch logins to use an authentication server; (b) turning on WPA3 at the access point; (c) a document prohibiting split tunneling; (d) disabling beacon frames on a WAP.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: name the four wireless configurations that hide the network, shrink the signal, encrypt the traffic, and block unknown devices, and cite each EK — that is 3.2.B.1 through B.4 in order. Question two is the policy-versus-configuration sort: for each item, say whether it is a managerial control or a configuration and cite the EK. The switch-login document is policy, 3.2.A.2; turning on WPA3 is configuration, 3.2.B.3; the split-tunneling document is policy, 3.2.A.3; disabling beacon frames is configuration, 3.2.B.1. Be precise and cite. Go.

slides.heading: Protecting Networks, in One Slide
points: Managerial controls are written policies that set a minimum standard: router, switch, VPN, and wireless (EK 3.2.A.1–A.4)
Router policy = firewall + no Telnet + auth server; switch policy = port security + MAC filtering + auth server (EK 3.2.A.1, A.2)
VPN policy = allowed roles + MFA + no split tunneling; wireless policy = EAP auth + AES encryption + disabled beacon frames (EK 3.2.A.3, A.4)
Configuring wireless = disable beacon frames, control signal direction/strength, enable WPA3, MAC filtering with authentication (EK 3.2.B.1–B.4)
WEP, WPS, and original WPA are insecure; WPA3 is currently the strongest wireless encryption algorithm (EK 3.2.B.3)
A policy (3.2.A) only protects once someone performs the configuration (3.2.B) that enforces it — you need both layers
slides.next: Topic 3.3 moves from these managerial controls to the technical controls that protect networks — the devices and mechanisms that defend traffic directly.
slides.script: Protecting networks, on one slide. Managerial controls are written policies that set a minimum standard — router, switch, VPN, and wireless. The router policy wants a firewall, no Telnet, and a central authentication server; the switch policy wants port security, MAC filtering, and that same authentication server. The VPN policy wants allowed roles, MFA, and no split tunneling; the wireless policy wants EAP authentication, AES encryption, and disabled beacon frames. Then configuring wireless is the hands-on half: disable beacon frames, control the signal, enable WPA3, and use MAC filtering with authentication. Remember WEP, WPS, and original WPA are insecure and WPA3 is the strongest. And the one big idea: a policy protects nothing until someone configures the feature that enforces it. Next topic, 3.3, we move from these managerial controls to the technical controls that defend the network directly.
